variants: Open your notes app right now and write down 5 niches you'd personally spend money in — no filtering, just list them.
Pull up a blank note and dump 5 product niches you'd actually buy from — gut instinct only, no second-guessing.
Write 5 niche markets you care about enough to buy from in your notes — raw and unfiltered, done in 2 minutes.
tasks.why: You can only sell well what you'd genuinely buy — start with your own conviction.

variants: Open Amazon Best Sellers right now and check the top 20 in each of your 5 niches — note which ones have under 500 reviews but strong sales rank.
Go to Amazon Best Sellers and scan the top 20 products in every niche you listed — flag any item with a weak review count but high rank position.
Browse the Amazon Best Sellers page for all 5 of your niches and screenshot every product ranked top 20 with fewer than 500 reviews.
tasks.why: Low review count + high rank = unmet demand — that's your entry point.

variants: Open TikTok, search each of your 5 niches with '#' + the niche name, and note the 3 most-viewed products you see in each — save the list.
Search TikTok for each niche you wrote down, watch the top 3 videos per niche, and record exactly what products are getting views.
Run TikTok searches on all 5 niches right now and write down which specific products are appearing in high-view content — be specific, not vague.
tasks.why: TikTok shows you what's already selling through organic buzz — find the proof before you pick.

variants: Look at yesterday's niche list and cross off any niche where you can't name at least 3 specific products you'd buy — keep only the real ones.
Review the 5 niches from Day 1 and eliminate any where you couldn't walk into a store and point at 3 products — ruthless cut.
Open your Day 1 notes and delete every niche that doesn't pass this test: would you spend your own $50 there in the next month?
tasks.why: Passion filters out the niches you'll quit on when it gets hard.

variants: Go to TikTok Shop and search your remaining niches — for each, sort by 'Most Sold' and write down the price points of the top 5 products.
Open TikTok Shop, search every surviving niche from your list, and record the exact price range of their bestsellers.
Pull up TikTok Shop for each of your remaining niches and note the specific dollar amounts on the top-selling products right now.
tasks.why: Price point research tells you what margins are possible before you commit.

variants: Pick your top 2 niches and search each one on Google Trends with a 12-month window — screenshot the trend lines and note whether each is rising, flat, or declining.
Open Google Trends, type in your top 2 remaining niches, set the time range to 12 months, and save screenshots of both graphs with your notes on trajectory.
Run your top 2 niches through Google Trends right now on a 12-month view — write one sentence describing the trend direction for each.
tasks.why: A declining trend means shrinking demand — confirm you're rowing with the current, not against it.

variants: Search your top 2 niches on Reddit right now — find the 2 most active subreddits for each and note their subscriber counts.
Pull up Reddit and find the main community for each of your top 2 niches — write down the subreddit name and size.
Open Reddit, search both niches, and identify the biggest active subreddit for each — jot the name and member count.
tasks.why: Active communities = active buyers — size and engagement confirm real demand.

variants: Read the top 10 posts from this week in each niche subreddit and write down the 3 most common complaints or product requests you see.
Scan this week's top posts in both niche subreddits and list the exact pain points or product wishes people are expressing.
Go through the top weekly posts in each subreddit and pull out 3 specific problems buyers are complaining about — write them word for word.
tasks.why: Customer complaints are product briefs — you're reading future ad copy right now.

variants: Search both niches on AliExpress, filter by 'Orders: Most' and find 3 products in each niche selling over 1,000 units — note the price and shipping time.
Open AliExpress for each niche, sort by most orders, and screenshot 3 products per niche that have crossed 1,000 sales with their pricing details.
Go to AliExpress, search your top 2 niches sorted by highest orders, and save the supplier page links for 3 high-volume products in each niche.
tasks.why: High AliExpress order counts confirm the product moves — you're verifying demand before investing.

variants: Look at all your research from Days 1-3 and pick ONE niche right now — write it at the top of a new note and commit to it.
Review your 3 days of research, choose a single niche, and write its name at the top of a fresh document — decision made, no revisiting.
Pull up your notes from Days 1-3, make the call on ONE niche, and type it at the top of a new page — your niche is locked.
tasks.why: Picking is the hardest part — the best niche you commit to beats the perfect niche you agonize over.

variants: Search your chosen niche on Shopify's store discovery at myshopify.com and find 3 existing stores — write down what products they lead with and their approximate price points.
Find 3 Shopify stores already selling in your niche by Googling '[niche] site:myshopify.com' and note their hero product and pricing.
Locate 3 active Shopify stores in your niche right now — examine each store's homepage and write down the first product they feature and its price.
tasks.why: Studying live competitors shows you what's already converting — reverse-engineer their wins.

variants: Go to the Facebook Ad Library, search your niche keyword, filter to 'Active' ads, and write down the 3 most common hooks you see in the first 10 ads.
Open Meta Ad Library at facebook.com/ads/library, search your niche, and screenshot the first 5 active ads — note what angle each one leads with.
Search your niche in the Facebook Ad Library right now and identify the 3 ad formats that appear most — write down the exact opening line or visual style.
tasks.why: Active ads are proof of paid profitability — what's running at scale is what's making money.

variants: Write a 1-sentence brand promise for your niche in this format: 'We sell [product] for [customer] who want [outcome] without [pain].'
Draft your brand's single-sentence promise right now using this structure: '[Product] built for [customer type] who are done with [pain point].'
Open your notes and write one sentence that captures what your store sells, who it's for, and what problem it solves — make it specific, not generic.
tasks.why: Your brand promise is your compass — every product decision filters through it.

variants: Search your top competitor's store name on SimilarWeb or Semrush and note their estimated monthly visitors and top traffic source.
Open SimilarWeb and paste the URL of the strongest competitor store you found yesterday — screenshot their traffic numbers and traffic channels.
Run your best competitor's Shopify URL through SimilarWeb right now and write down their monthly visits, top country, and primary traffic source.
tasks.why: Understanding where competitors get traffic tells you exactly which channels to prioritize.

variants: Go to CJ Dropshipping, search your niche, and find 3 products with at least 4-star ratings, under $15 cost, and delivery under 15 days — save the product links.
Open CJDropshipping.com, search your niche category, filter by rating above 4 stars and cost under $15, and list 3 candidate products with their logistics details.
Search your niche on CJ Dropshipping now and identify 3 products that cost under $15 with shipping under 2 weeks — copy the product page URLs into your notes.
tasks.why: Your unit economics live or die on supplier costs and shipping times — lock in winners early.

variants: Search Google for '[your niche] + Reddit problem' and read the first 3 results — write down 1 product feature buyers keep asking for.
Search '[niche] complaints' on Google and skim the first page — pull out the single most repeated customer frustration in a sentence.
Go to Google and search '[your niche] + forum + best product' — skim the top 3 results and write down the #1 product feature that comes up repeatedly.
tasks.why: The most-requested feature becomes your product angle — let buyers write your positioning.

variants: Open Amazon, find your top product candidate, read the 1-star reviews, and write down the 3 most common complaints in exact customer language.
Search your hero product on Amazon, sort reviews by 1-star, and pull out 3 specific complaints customers repeat — write them in the customer's own words.
Go to Amazon, find your strongest product candidate, filter to lowest-rated reviews, and extract 3 repeated problems using the exact phrasing customers use.
tasks.why: 1-star reviews are your product improvement brief and your future ad copy — mine them now.

variants: Go to Alibaba.com, search your chosen product, message 2 suppliers using their built-in chat asking for MOQ, unit price at 50 units, and sample cost — send both messages before closing.
Open Alibaba, find 2 suppliers for your product, and send each a message today asking their minimum order, price per unit for 50 pcs, and whether samples are available.
Search your product on Alibaba right now, pick 2 suppliers with 'Trade Assurance' badges, and message both asking for MOQ, 50-unit pricing, and sample availability.
tasks.why: Supplier outreach starts the relationship — you can't ship anything without a source.

variants: Review your notes from Days 1-6 and write 3 bullet points: what you've confirmed about your niche, what's still unclear, and one thing you'd do differently.
Open your research notes from the past 6 days and write down: your strongest validation signal, your biggest open question, and one assumption you've already disproved.
Do a 3-minute review of Days 1-6 — write one sentence for what's solid, one for what needs more work, and one for what surprised you.
tasks.why: Weekly reflection locks in learning and prevents you from carrying bad assumptions forward.

variants: Score your chosen niche 1-10 on these 3 criteria: passion (would you use it?), profit (margins above 3x?), proof (saw it selling?) — write the scores and one sentence each.
Rate your niche on passion, margin potential, and market proof using a 1-10 scale — write each score with a one-line justification right now.
Grade your niche on 3 dimensions: personal interest, unit economics potential, and real-world sales evidence — give each a number and explain why in one sentence.
tasks.why: Scoring forces you to be honest — low scores in week 1 are easier to fix than low scores in month 3.

variants: Write a 150-word 'niche case' — your product, target customer, price point, supplier source, and one line on why this wins — as if pitching to a partner.
Type out a concise niche summary covering: what you sell, who buys it, what it costs to source, what you'll charge, and what makes it better than current options.
Draft a brief paragraph summarizing your niche decision: product type, target buyer, sourcing plan, retail price, and your competitive angle — get it all in one paragraph.
tasks.why: Writing the case makes your thinking concrete — if you can't explain it in a paragraph, you haven't decided yet.

variants: Write 5 possible store names for your niche brand right now — no domain checking yet, just brainstorm fast.
Dump 5 brand name ideas for your store in your notes — 2 minutes, no filtering, write whatever comes to mind.
Set a 2-minute timer and write 5 potential names for your ecommerce brand — speed over perfection, editing comes later.
tasks.why: Getting names on paper fast prevents name-paralysis — you need a working name, not a perfect one.

variants: Go to Namecheap.com, check all 5 name ideas for .com availability, and highlight the available ones — pick your top available option.
Open Namecheap, search all 5 of your brand names for .com domains, and note which ones are available and what they cost.
Check every name on your list at Namecheap right now for .com availability — cross off any that are taken and circle your best available option.
tasks.why: Domain availability narrows your choices fast — don't fall in love with a name before checking.

variants: Go to shopify.com, start the free trial, enter your chosen brand name as the store name, and complete the basic setup questionnaire — your store URL should exist when you're done.
Open Shopify's signup page right now, create your account, name your store, and complete the onboarding steps until you reach the dashboard.
Sign up for Shopify's trial at shopify.com using your chosen brand name — go through every setup step until your store dashboard is live and visible.
tasks.why: Your store existing is the first physical proof your brand is real — don't delay this.

variants: Log into your Shopify store and complete the basic settings: store name, currency, timezone, and your contact email — all 4 done before you close the tab.
Open your Shopify dashboard and fill in the store settings: display name, time zone, currency, and business email — do all four right now.
Go into Shopify Settings today and confirm your store name, currency (set to USD or your target market), timezone, and contact email are all correctly filled in.
tasks.why: Correct settings prevent checkout errors later — fix them now before a customer hits a broken experience.

variants: Search the Shopify App Store for a free dropshipping connector — install DSers or AutoDS on your store and complete the account link before closing.
Open Shopify's App Store, find DSers (the AliExpress connector), install it on your store, and link your AliExpress account in the setup wizard.
Install DSers on your Shopify store right now from the app marketplace and connect it to your AliExpress account — complete the full connection, not just the install.
tasks.why: DSers is how your orders flow automatically from store to supplier — it has to be set up before you add products.

variants: Find your best product candidate on AliExpress, open it in DSers, and import it to your Shopify store — then set the retail price at 3x the AliExpress cost.
Go into DSers, search your chosen product, click 'Push to Shopify', and once it's imported set the price to at least 3x what you're paying the supplier.
Import your first product from AliExpress into your Shopify store via DSers right now, then open the product editor and price it at a 3x markup.
tasks.why: Your first imported product makes the store feel real — pricing at 3x gives you room for ads and refunds.

variants: Open your product page in Shopify and rewrite the product title to include the key benefit in 6 words or fewer — no supplier gibberish.
Edit your product title right now — delete the AliExpress default name and write a clean, benefit-first title under 8 words.
Go to your Shopify product page and replace the imported title with a punchy, customer-facing name that leads with the outcome, not the feature.
tasks.why: Your title is the first thing shoppers read — supplier names are ugly and generic, yours needs to sell.

variants: Write a 4-bullet product description for your Shopify listing: line 1 = headline benefit, line 2 = key feature, line 3 = use case, line 4 = trust signal — done before you close the tab.
Open your Shopify product editor and write 4 bullets: the main outcome, one standout feature, the ideal use scenario, and one credibility line — all four, right now.
Replace your product description with exactly 4 bullet points today: what it does, what makes it different, who it's for, and one reason to trust it.
tasks.why: Four tight bullets convert better than a paragraph — structure your copy like a shopper scans.

variants: Download 5 supplier product images, open Canva, remove any watermarks or logos, and upload them as your product gallery — at least 5 clean images in the listing before you stop.
Take the best 5 images from your AliExpress supplier, clean them up in Canva by removing text overlays, and upload them to your Shopify product gallery.
Open Canva, grab 5 of the cleanest supplier images for your product, remove any Chinese text or watermarks, and upload all 5 to your Shopify listing now.
tasks.why: Clean, watermark-free images are the minimum bar — anything less and shoppers bounce immediately.

variants: Go to your Shopify Payments setup and enable the payment gateway for your country — enter your banking details and submit for approval.
Open Shopify Settings > Payments right now and complete the Shopify Payments activation form — you can't take money until this is done.
Navigate to Payments in your Shopify dashboard and activate your payment processor by filling in all required fields — do it in one sitting.
tasks.why: No payment gateway = no sales — this is the most critical infrastructure step.

variants: Set up your shipping zones in Shopify: create one zone for your home country with free shipping over $50, and one international zone — rates set, not empty.
Go to Shopify Settings > Shipping and add at least 2 shipping zones with real rates — your home country and at least one other region.
Open Shipping settings in Shopify and build out your zones now: define rates for domestic and at least one international market before closing the tab.
tasks.why: Undefined shipping stops checkout cold — set real rates before you drive a single visitor.

variants: Go to your Shopify store's public URL, add your product to cart, and complete a test purchase using Shopify's Bogus Gateway — confirm the order appears in your admin.
Run a full checkout test on your store right now using Shopify's test payment mode — add to cart, enter fake card details from Shopify docs, and verify the order lands in your dashboard.
Enable Shopify's Bogus Gateway in settings, go to your storefront, put your product in cart, and complete checkout — confirm you see the order in your Orders tab.
tasks.why: A broken checkout is invisible until a real customer hits it — test it yourself before it costs you.

variants: Write a 3-sentence 'About Us' page for your store: who you are, who you serve, and why this product — add it to Shopify's pages section.
Create your About page in Shopify right now — 3 sentences max: your story, your customer, and the one thing that makes your store worth buying from.
Open Shopify > Pages, create an About page, and write exactly 3 sentences: one about who you are, one about who you help, one about what makes you different.
tasks.why: An About page builds trust — it converts skeptical browsers into first-time buyers.

variants: Add a Refund Policy and Privacy Policy to your Shopify store using the auto-generate tool in Settings > Policies — publish both before closing.
Go to Shopify Settings > Policies and use the auto-generator to create your refund policy and privacy policy — save and publish both now.
Open Shopify's policy generator right now, create your refund and privacy policy pages, and publish them — your store is incomplete without these.
tasks.why: Missing policies get stores flagged by ad platforms and scare buyers — get them live today.

variants: Create a business Instagram account for your brand right now — use your store name, upload a logo or clean product image as your profile pic, and write a 2-line bio with your store URL.
Sign up for an Instagram business account under your brand name, upload a product photo as your profile image, and fill in your bio with what you sell and your Shopify URL.
Open Instagram, create a new business account with your store name, set your profile photo, write a bio that says what you sell and who it's for, and paste your store link.
tasks.why: Your social presence is part of your brand trust stack — a blank profile kills credibility.

variants: Create a TikTok business account under your brand name right now — fill in the bio, add your store link in the website field, and upload a profile photo.
Open TikTok, create a new account for your brand, complete your profile (bio, link, photo), and switch it to a Business account in settings.
Sign up for TikTok under your brand name, write a 2-line bio describing your niche, paste your Shopify URL, and set a clean profile image — all done before you exit.
tasks.why: TikTok will be your primary organic traffic source — set up the channel before you need it.

variants: Search your niche on TikTok and watch 10 videos that have over 50K views — write down the exact hook line from the first 3 seconds of each.
Open TikTok, search your niche, and watch the top 10 videos sorted by views — screenshot or write down the opening 3 seconds of each, specifically the first sentence spoken or shown.
Find the 10 most-viewed TikToks in your niche right now and note the hook format each one uses — is it a question, a bold claim, a before/after reveal? List all 10.
tasks.why: Hooks are where views die or explode — study 10 winners before you shoot your first video.

variants: Film and post your first TikTok right now — hold the product (or a relevant item), speak one benefit out loud, and upload it with 3 niche hashtags. Done is better than perfect.
Record a 15-30 second TikTok today showing your product or niche context — say one clear thing it does, add 3 relevant hashtags, and hit publish before you talk yourself out of it.
Post your very first TikTok for your brand today — show the product, say one sentence about who it's for, and post it with at least 3 niche hashtags — raw and real is fine.
tasks.why: Your first TikTok breaks the psychological barrier — nothing matters more than posting your first one.

variants: Pull up your Day 7 niche case and compare it to where you are now — write 2 things that have changed or that you now know to be different.
Open your Day 7 notes and review your niche case paragraph — update any assumption that's been proven wrong or confirmed by your last 7 days of work.
Go back to your Day 7 summary and mark which assumptions still hold and which you'd now change — write the updated version in 2 sentences.
tasks.why: Two-week reviews catch drift early — your assumptions need to evolve with what you've built.

variants: Review your TikTok account insights from Day 13's first video — note the view count, profile visits, and completion rate, then write one thing you'd change next time.
Open your TikTok creator dashboard for your first post, read the analytics, and write down the views, watch time, and the single biggest change you'll make on the next video.
Check the stats on your Day 13 TikTok right now — views, completion rate, shares — and write one specific improvement you'll make to the next video based on what you see.
tasks.why: Analyzing your first video before making the second one is how you improve without guessing.

variants: Do a full store audit: visit your Shopify store as a customer, click Add to Cart, go all the way to checkout, and write down every friction point or broken element you find.
Open your Shopify store URL on your phone right now, act like a first-time buyer, and note every confusing step, missing element, or ugly page between landing and checkout.
Browse your own store on mobile from the homepage through checkout and write down 3 specific things a real customer would hesitate or quit over — then fix at least 1 of them now.
tasks.why: You are your first mystery shopper — find the holes before your first customer does.

variants: Fix the top friction point you found in yesterday's store audit — open Shopify, make the edit, and confirm it looks right on mobile.
Go into Shopify right now and fix the #1 problem you identified in your Day 14 store walkthrough — don't start anything new until that's corrected.
Address the biggest issue from your store audit yesterday — log in, make the change, and view it on your phone to confirm it's fixed.
tasks.why: Your audit was only useful if it leads to fixes — start each session by resolving yesterday's blocker.

variants: Go to your Shopify theme editor and install a free product review app (Judge.me or Loox free tier) — enable it on your product page before you close.
Open Shopify App Store, search 'product reviews', install Judge.me for free, and activate the review widget on your product listing today.
Add a review app to your store right now — install Judge.me or Loox from the Shopify App Store, configure it, and verify the widget shows on your product page.
tasks.why: Review widgets increase conversion by up to 30% — install it before you send any traffic.

variants: Film and post your second TikTok today using one of the hook styles you wrote down on Day 13 — apply your Day 14 lesson and aim for a 10% longer watch time.
Record your Day 15 TikTok using a hook format that performed well in your niche research — apply the one improvement you noted after watching your first post's stats.
Post TikTok video #2 today — use a hook from your Day 13 research, implement the change you identified on Day 14, and post it before 6pm.
tasks.why: Daily TikTok posting compounds — video 2 built on video 1's lesson is already better than most.

variants: Write 7 TikTok video concepts for your niche — just a one-line idea for each, not scripts — done in 2 minutes.
Brainstorm 7 video ideas for your brand's TikTok in 2 minutes — one sentence per idea, no overthinking, just a list.
Set a timer for 2 minutes and write 7 TikTok content ideas for your niche — hooks, demos, comparisons, testimonials — anything goes.
tasks.why: A content idea bank prevents the blank-screen paralysis that kills daily posting.

variants: Post today's TikTok using one of the 7 ideas you just brainstormed — cross it off the list when it's live.
Pick one idea from your list, record it in under 5 minutes, and post it to TikTok — mark it done on your content list.
Choose the easiest idea from your 7-idea list, film it right now, and get it posted — your content bank is only useful if you execute from it.
tasks.why: Three days of posting in a row starts building algorithmic momentum on TikTok.

variants: Search your niche on TikTok, find 10 accounts posting similar content, and follow all 10 from your brand account — then like and comment on 2 posts from each.
Identify 10 TikTok creators in your niche right now, follow them from your brand account, and leave a genuine comment on 2 of each creator's videos.
Go to TikTok, search your niche, find 10 active accounts, follow each, and comment something specific (not just emojis) on 2 posts from each — 20 total comments.
tasks.why: Engaging with niche creators signals relevance to the algorithm and starts building community.

variants: Write your product's 3 strongest benefits as 3 separate sentences — no features, only outcomes the customer gets.
Open your notes and write 3 sentences describing what your product does for the customer — each sentence must name an outcome, not a feature.
Draft 3 benefit statements for your hero product right now: each must start with 'You'll' or 'Get' and describe a real-world result, not a spec.
tasks.why: Benefit-first copy is what converts — separate features from outcomes before writing any more ads or copy.

variants: Post your 4th TikTok today using one of your 3 benefits as the hook — say the benefit out loud in the first 2 seconds.
Film and post a TikTok where your opening line is one of your 3 product benefits — lead with the outcome, show the product second.
Use one benefit statement from today's writing as your TikTok hook — record it, post it, and note which benefit you led with.
tasks.why: Benefit-first hooks stop the scroll — test one today and see how it performs vs. your earlier videos.

variants: Search your niche product on Amazon, open the top 3 listings, and copy the exact phrases customers use in 5-star reviews — paste them into a 'Voice of Customer' note.
Go to Amazon, pull up the top 3 products in your niche, and extract real customer phrases from 5-star reviews — write down at least 5 exact quotes.
Open Amazon, find 3 bestselling products in your niche, read 5-star reviews on each, and build a list of the 5 most powerful customer phrases — exact wording, not your summary.
tasks.why: Customers tell you how to sell to them — their own words in your ads convert better than anything you'd write.

variants: Add your store link to your Instagram bio if it's not there already, then post a product photo with a 2-sentence caption and 5 niche hashtags.
Open your brand Instagram account, post one clean product image today with a caption that uses one of your benefit statements from yesterday, and include 5 hashtags.
Post your first Instagram content today — one product photo, one benefit-driven caption using real customer language from your Day 17 research, and 5 relevant hashtags.
tasks.why: A second organic channel multiplies your discovery surface — Instagram + TikTok together outperform either alone.

variants: Post your 5th TikTok today and try a different format from your previous 4 — if you've been showing the product, try a voiceover comparison; if you've been talking, try text-on-screen.
Record and post TikTok #5 today using a format you haven't tried yet — switch from whatever your default has been and test something new.
Make your Day 18 TikTok deliberately different from your last 4 — change the format, the hook style, or the visual approach and post it today.
tasks.why: Format variety surfaces which style the algorithm rewards for your specific audience.

variants: Go back to the supplier replies you requested on Day 6 — if you haven't heard back, send follow-up messages to both; if you have, save the quotes to a spreadsheet with columns: supplier name, MOQ, unit cost, sample cost, shipping days.
Check your Alibaba inbox for supplier responses from Day 6, follow up on any that haven't replied, and organize all quotes into a simple spreadsheet with price and timeline columns.
Open Alibaba messages now and review your Day 6 supplier contacts — reply to those who responded asking for a sample, and send a follow-up to those who went silent.
tasks.why: Supplier relationships require follow-up — no response doesn't mean no interest.

variants: Write a 30-word product headline for your Shopify store homepage — it must answer: who it's for, what it does, and why now.
Draft your store's hero headline in under 30 words — it needs to tell a visitor in 5 seconds exactly who should buy this and why.
Open Shopify's theme editor and write a new homepage headline that covers: your target customer, the product outcome, and one urgency or specificity hook — 30 words max.
tasks.why: Your homepage headline has 5 seconds to earn a scroll — make every word work.

variants: Update your Shopify homepage in the theme editor: add your new headline, add a product image above the fold, and make sure the 'Shop Now' button is visible without scrolling on mobile.
Open your Shopify theme editor and redesign the homepage top section: headline, product image, and call-to-action button all visible on mobile before any scroll.
Go into your Shopify theme and fix your above-the-fold section today — your headline, a clean image, and a visible CTA must all load without scrolling on an iPhone.
tasks.why: Above-the-fold design determines if visitors stay or bounce — this is your store's first impression.

variants: Post your 6th TikTok today — this time film it as a 'problem + solution' structure: show the problem in the first 5 seconds, introduce the product as the fix.
Record and post TikTok #6 in a problem-solution format — open with a relatable pain in your niche, then reveal your product as the answer.
Film today's TikTok using the before/after framework: 5 seconds of 'the problem your customer has', then your product as the solution — post it today.
tasks.why: Problem-solution is the highest-converting TikTok format — test it now before you put money behind it.

variants: Open your TikTok analytics dashboard and write down your views, followers, and completion rate for each of your 6 videos — sort them from highest to lowest views.
Check TikTok creator analytics for all 6 of your videos right now — rank them by view count and note the completion rate on the top 2.
Pull up your TikTok account analytics, find the stats for all 6 posts, and write them in a ranked list by view count — note what the top 2 have in common.
tasks.why: Six videos is enough data to see a pattern — what's working is what you double down on.

variants: Film and post TikTok #7 using the hook format from your best-performing video so far — replicate the structure, not the content.
Record today's TikTok using the same hook style as your #1 video from analytics — different product angle, same structural formula.
Post your Day 20 TikTok modeled after your highest-view video — same hook format, same pacing, new content angle.
tasks.why: Double down on what's working — copying your own winners is smarter than guessing.

variants: Order a sample of your hero product from your top supplier choice today — pay for it, get a confirmation, and write the expected delivery date in your notes.
Go to AliExpress or your confirmed supplier and place a sample order for your main product right now — complete payment and save the order number.
Order one sample unit of your product from the best supplier in your notes today — complete the transaction and screenshot the confirmation.
tasks.why: You can't review quality, film real content, or approve product until you hold it — order the sample today.

variants: Review the past 3 weeks: write 3 things you've built (store, social, supplier), 3 things still missing, and the one single task that would move the needle most this week.
Do a 3-week check-in: open your notes and list what's live, what's unfinished, and what's blocking your first sale — write all three in under 4 minutes.
Audit your 21-day progress right now — name 3 completed wins, 3 open gaps, and one priority that would most accelerate your path to a first sale.
tasks.why: 21-day review breaks bad momentum before it compounds — you need an honest score before starting week 4.

variants: Post TikTok #8 today — try a 'unboxing or product reveal' format if your sample hasn't arrived, film a setup or behind-the-scenes of what building the store looks like.
Record and post today's TikTok showing behind-the-scenes of building your store or prepping for launch — authenticity hooks on TikTok, use it.
Film TikTok #8 as a 'day in the life of launching a brand' style — raw, real, relatable — and post it before you move on to anything else.
tasks.why: Behind-the-scenes content builds audience before your store is ready — future buyers are watching now.
